top5Opportunities.statement: Minimize the likelihood of Clery Act compliance gaps due to missing door event records from campus exit devices that experienced intermittent wireless outages
Minimize the risk of regulatory fine exposure from undocumented emergency exit non-compliance events discovered during unannounced NFPA or state fire marshal audits
Minimize the likelihood of incomplete emergency exit coverage mapping that leaves fire-code-mandated campus exits unmonitored after initial deployment
Minimize the time to detect and route an unauthorized emergency exit opening event to the campus security operations center
Minimize the likelihood of RF dead zones in concrete campus buildings causing emergency exit devices to transmit outside effective receiver range

needs.statement: Minimize the likelihood of incomplete emergency exit coverage mapping that leaves fire-code-mandated campus exits unmonitored after initial deployment
needs.importanceRationale: Fire code mandates monitoring of all emergency exits; incomplete coverage creates regulatory liability and leaves campus security gaps at unmonitored doors.
needs.satisfactionRationale: Basic prop alarms (64% share) are deployed ad hoc without systematic coverage mapping. Electronic exit control covers primary exits but cost limits extend to secondary doors.
needs.primaryStakeholder: Campus Security Officer / Facility Security Manager
stakeholderIds: Campus Security Officer / Facility Security Manager

needs.statement: Minimize the effort required to assess door hardware compatibility and wireless signal reach across heterogeneous campus building stock before device selection
needs.importanceRationale: Campus building stock spans decades of construction standards; door hardware varies widely, making pre-deployment assessment essential to avoid costly rework.
needs.satisfactionRationale: Integrators conduct site surveys as standard practice. Electronic exit control vendors provide compatibility matrices. Assessment effort is manageable but not trivial.
needs.primaryStakeholder: Campus Security Officer / Facility Security Manager
stakeholderIds: Campus Security Officer / Facility Security Manager

needs.statement: Minimize the likelihood of RF dead zones in concrete campus buildings causing emergency exit devices to transmit outside effective receiver range
needs.importanceRationale: University buildings frequently use reinforced concrete construction that severely attenuates wireless signals, creating monitoring blind spots.
needs.satisfactionRationale: Basic prop alarms (64%) are local-only and unaffected. Wireless IoT systems have limited RF planning tools. Post-deployment dead zone discovery is common.
needs.primaryStakeholder: Campus Security Officer / Facility Security Manager
stakeholderIds: Campus Security Officer / Facility Security Manager

needs.statement: Minimize installation labor-hours required to deploy exit monitoring devices across a multi-building campus without licensed electrical contractor involvement
needs.importanceRationale: Campus-wide deployment across dozens of buildings requires significant labor; electrician involvement adds scheduling constraints and cost.
needs.satisfactionRationale: Basic prop alarms install without electrical work. Electronic exit control requires licensed electricians. Wireless IoT systems are self-install. Market-weighted satisfaction is moderate.
needs.primaryStakeholder: Installer / Facilities Maintenance Tech

needs.statement: Minimize variability in alarm guard installation quality across lever-handle, panic-bar, and glass-frame door configurations during a campus-wide rollout
needs.importanceRationale: Inconsistent installation quality across door types leads to false alarms, missed events, and reliability variance across campus.
needs.satisfactionRationale: Basic prop alarms are simple to install consistently. Surface-mount guards accommodate multiple door types. Installation quality is adequate for most configurations.
needs.primaryStakeholder: Installer / Facilities Maintenance Tech

needs.statement: Minimize the likelihood of deploying exit alarm devices with undetected wireless connectivity failures that are not discovered until after campus go-live
needs.importanceRationale: Post-deployment connectivity failures mean exits appear monitored but events are not transmitted — a silent security gap.
needs.satisfactionRationale: Basic commissioning tests exist but are not comprehensive. Wireless IoT systems lack automated pre-go-live connectivity verification across all deployed units.
needs.primaryStakeholder: Installer / Facilities Maintenance Tech

needs.statement: Minimize the effort required to verify wireless transmission integrity between each newly installed device and the central receiver before signing off on commissioning
needs.importanceRationale: Commissioning sign-off without verified transmission integrity creates accountability risk for the installer and downstream monitoring reliability issues.
needs.satisfactionRationale: Manual walk-test procedures exist. Some wireless systems have learn-mode verification. Process is adequate but labor-intensive for large campus deployments.
needs.primaryStakeholder: Installer / Facilities Maintenance Tech

needs.statement: Minimize the likelihood of alarm guard mechanical failure in high-traffic campus exit environments (dormitories, student unions) causing an unguarded door state
needs.importanceRationale: High-traffic dorm and student union exits experience thousands of activations annually; mechanical failure leaves the exit unprotected.
needs.satisfactionRationale: Basic prop alarms (64%) are mechanically simple and reliable. Incumbents adequately meet the mechanical reliability need; satisfaction = 5 (not underserved).
needs.primaryStakeholder: Campus Security Officer / Facility Security Manager
stakeholderIds: Campus Security Officer / Facility Security Manager

needs.statement: Minimize the likelihood of false alarm triggers caused by accidental door contact disrupting normal building operations
needs.importanceRationale: False alarms in high-traffic campus environments cause alarm fatigue and disruption to lectures, events, and operations.
needs.satisfactionRationale: All incumbent technologies experience false alarm issues from accidental contact, wind, and thermal expansion. No event-type differentiation exists in basic systems.
needs.primaryStakeholder: Campus Security Officer / Facility Security Manager
stakeholderIds: Campus Security Officer / Facility Security Manager

needs.statement: Minimize the time to detect and route an unauthorized emergency exit opening event to the campus security operations center
needs.importanceRationale: Rapid detection of unauthorized exits is critical for campus safety, especially during lockdown scenarios and after-hours security.
needs.satisfactionRationale: Basic prop alarms (64%) produce local sound only with zero central routing. Electronic exit control routes to panels but has limited mobile push. Patrol-based detection has multi-hour gaps.
needs.primaryStakeholder: Campus Security Officer / Facility Security Manager
stakeholderIds: Campus Security Officer / Facility Security Manager

needs.statement: Minimize the number of door events failing to reach the central monitoring dashboard due to RF signal attenuation in reinforced-concrete campus buildings
needs.importanceRationale: Lost door events create gaps in the compliance record and reduce security situational awareness. Each missed event is an unrecorded potential breach.
needs.satisfactionRationale: Basic prop alarms have no central transmission at all. Wireless IoT systems experience RF losses in concrete buildings. Event loss detection and recovery are not standard features.
needs.primaryStakeholder: Campus Security Officer / Facility Security Manager
stakeholderIds: Campus Security Officer / Facility Security Manager

needs.statement: Minimize variability in battery depletion rates across devices deployed in buildings with different door-use frequencies and ambient temperatures
needs.importanceRationale: Unpredictable battery depletion makes proactive maintenance scheduling difficult across a campus with varying usage patterns.
needs.satisfactionRationale: No wireless exit alarm system adjusts battery life estimates based on actual usage or environmental conditions. Scheduled replacement relies on conservative uniform assumptions.
needs.primaryStakeholder: Campus Security Officer / Facility Security Manager
stakeholderIds: Campus Security Officer / Facility Security Manager

needs.statement: Minimize the time between a campus emergency exit device losing wireless connectivity and the security operations center detecting the monitoring gap
needs.importanceRationale: A connectivity gap means events at that door are not transmitted; delayed detection extends the unmonitored window.
needs.satisfactionRationale: Basic prop alarms (64%) have no connectivity to lose. Wireless systems rarely implement heartbeat monitoring for gap detection. Connectivity loss may go undetected for days.
needs.primaryStakeholder: Campus Security Officer / Facility Security Manager
stakeholderIds: Campus Security Officer / Facility Security Manager

needs.statement: Increase the ability to monitor all campus emergency exit devices from a single security dashboard without requiring building-specific logins or separate hardware systems
needs.importanceRationale: Campus security operates centrally; building-specific systems fragment situational awareness and slow response times.
needs.satisfactionRationale: Basic prop alarms have no dashboard. Electronic exit control is building-specific. No incumbent offers a unified multi-building campus dashboard for exit monitoring.
needs.primaryStakeholder: Campus Security Officer / Facility Security Manager
stakeholderIds: Campus Security Officer / Facility Security Manager

needs.statement: Minimize the time required to replace an exit alarm guard battery without triggering the alarm horn or temporarily disabling the door's fire-code escape path
needs.importanceRationale: Battery replacement that triggers the alarm disrupts building operations; disabling the escape path even temporarily violates fire code.
needs.satisfactionRationale: Most devices allow key-bypass during battery replacement. The process is manageable but requires trained maintenance staff with appropriate access credentials.
needs.primaryStakeholder: Campus Security Officer / Facility Security Manager
stakeholderIds: Campus Security Officer / Facility Security Manager

needs.statement: Minimize the time to restore full exit alarm and wireless monitoring function after a device fault or battery exhaustion event
needs.importanceRationale: Extended restoration time leaves the exit unmonitored; each hour of downtime is an unprotected exposure window.
needs.satisfactionRationale: Battery replacement is straightforward but device fault diagnosis requires integrator support. Wireless re-commissioning adds delay. Restoration time varies from hours to days.
needs.primaryStakeholder: Campus Security Officer / Facility Security Manager
stakeholderIds: Campus Security Officer / Facility Security Manager

needs.statement: Minimize the labor required to compile emergency exit compliance documentation for annual fire safety inspections and Clery Act Annual Security Report filings
needs.importanceRationale: Clery Act requires annual security reports including exit compliance data; manual compilation consumes significant staff hours during audit preparation.
needs.satisfactionRationale: Basic prop alarms (64%) produce zero digital records. Manual log compilation from disparate sources is the dominant practice. Automated compliance documentation is essentially nonexistent.
needs.primaryStakeholder: VP Facilities / Chief Security Officer

needs.statement: Minimize the likelihood of Clery Act compliance gaps due to missing door event records from campus exit devices that experienced intermittent wireless outages
needs.importanceRationale: Clery Act violations carry federal fines up to $69,733 per violation; missing event records create prima facie compliance gaps.
needs.satisfactionRationale: Wireless outages are silent — events during outage periods are permanently lost. No incumbent system provides outage-resilient local buffering or gap detection alerts.
needs.primaryStakeholder: VP Facilities / Chief Security Officer

needs.statement: Minimize the risk of regulatory fine exposure from undocumented emergency exit non-compliance events discovered during unannounced NFPA or state fire marshal audits
needs.importanceRationale: Unannounced fire marshal audits can result in immediate corrective action requirements, occupancy restrictions, and financial penalties.
needs.satisfactionRationale: Documentation gaps are widespread across all incumbent technologies. Basic prop alarms produce zero records. Wired panels provide partial logging. No automated compliance reporting exists.
needs.primaryStakeholder: VP Facilities / Chief Security Officer

needs.statement: Increase the ability to scale emergency exit monitoring incrementally across a campus building-by-building without system-wide infrastructure changes or full re-commissioning
needs.importanceRationale: University budgets are allocated annually by building or zone; system-wide upfront investment is often not feasible.
needs.satisfactionRationale: Basic prop alarms are inherently building-independent. Electronic exit control requires per-building infrastructure. Incremental scaling is partially supported by wireless systems.
needs.primaryStakeholder: Campus Security Officer / Facility Security Manager
stakeholderIds: Campus Security Officer / Facility Security Manager

needs.statement: Minimize the effort required to evaluate exit monitoring vendor interoperability with existing campus PSIM and Building Management Systems before procurement commitment
needs.importanceRationale: Campuses invest heavily in PSIM/BMS infrastructure; exit monitoring must interoperate or it creates operational silos.
needs.satisfactionRationale: Vendor interoperability evaluation requires custom integration testing. No standardized compatibility framework exists for exit monitoring systems.
needs.primaryStakeholder: Campus Security Officer / Facility Security Manager
stakeholderIds: Campus Security Officer / Facility Security Manager

needs.statement: Minimize the likelihood of Clery Act Annual Security Report inaccuracies caused by manual compilation of door-event records that were not captured in a central digital log
needs.importanceRationale: Inaccurate Clery Act reports expose institutions to federal investigation and reputational damage.
needs.satisfactionRationale: Manual compilation from paper logs and disparate systems introduces transcription errors. No central digital event log exists in 80%+ of campus exit monitoring deployments.
needs.primaryStakeholder: VP Facilities / Chief Security Officer

needs.statement: Minimize the likelihood of wireless signal attenuation below reliable reporting threshold in reinforced-concrete campus buildings where structural floors and walls exceed the 30 m EasyWave integrated transmitter range
needs.importanceRationale: Reinforced concrete is standard in university construction; EasyWave's 30 m range is insufficient for many building configurations.
needs.satisfactionRationale: Repeater infrastructure mitigates but adds cost and complexity. No automatic range verification exists during deployment.
needs.primaryStakeholder: Installer / Facilities Maintenance Tech

needs.statement: Minimize the likelihood of wireless coverage gaps discovered only after campus-wide deployment, requiring unplanned repeater infrastructure additions that increase per-door cost and delay full-campus monitoring go-live
needs.importanceRationale: Post-deployment coverage gap discovery increases total cost of ownership and delays campus-wide protection timelines.
needs.satisfactionRationale: RF site surveys can reduce surprises but are not always conducted. Coverage gap discovery post-deployment is a known risk with wireless exit monitoring.
needs.primaryStakeholder: Installer / Facilities Maintenance Tech

needs.statement: Minimize the likelihood of IoT battery exhaustion on dormitory emergency exits where 24/7 student occupancy and high door-event frequency depletes a 9V block battery before the scheduled 2-year maintenance cycle
needs.importanceRationale: Dormitory exits see 24/7 traffic with high activation frequency; accelerated battery drain is predictable but unmonitored in current systems.
needs.satisfactionRationale: No wireless exit alarm system adjusts battery life estimates for high-traffic doors. The 2-year target assumes low-traffic installations.
needs.primaryStakeholder: Installer / Facilities Maintenance Tech

needs.statement: Minimize the integration effort required to relay EasyWave alarm events into campus PSIM or building automation platforms standardized on BACnet, KNX, or IP-based protocols via GfS-proprietary BUS gateway
needs.importanceRationale: Campus PSIM platforms are standardized on open protocols; proprietary gateway integration creates vendor lock-in and ongoing maintenance burden.
needs.satisfactionRationale: EasyWave-to-BACnet/KNX bridging requires a proprietary GfS BUS gateway intermediary. No native open-protocol output exists on the transmitter.
needs.primaryStakeholder: Installer / Facilities Maintenance Tech

needs.statement: Minimize the likelihood of door-event timestamps logged by the IoT monitoring system creating FERPA or EU GDPR compliance exposure when access event records are attributable to identifiable resident students
needs.importanceRationale: FERPA and GDPR impose strict requirements on student data; identifiable access logs create regulatory exposure if not properly managed.
needs.satisfactionRationale: Most exit monitoring systems log anonymous events without student identification. Risk is moderate and manageable through system configuration.
needs.primaryStakeholder: Installer / Facilities Maintenance Tech

needs.statement: Minimize the likelihood of surface-mounted exit alarm guard housings being physically tampered with or dislodged in student dormitory corridors, leaving an emergency exit in an unguarded state without a monitoring system alert
needs.importanceRationale: Student dormitory corridors experience intentional and accidental tampering; surface-mounted housings at handle height are physically accessible.
needs.satisfactionRationale: Anti-tamper housings exist but are not standard. Basic mounting provides adequate resistance against casual contact but not determined manipulation.
needs.primaryStakeholder: Installer / Facilities Maintenance Tech
